title: Pulled pork
subtitle: Slow-roasted pork shoulder rubbed with spices and pulled into tender shreds
excerpt: A whole bone-in pork shoulder rubbed with a brown sugar and spice crust, slow-roasted at low heat for 6 to 8 hours until the meat pulls away from the bone in soft, yielding shreds. Served in soft rolls with coleslaw and barbecue sauce. The slow cooking does the work; the technique requires patience more than skill.
sourceNotes: Pulled pork is a staple of American barbecue tradition, particularly in the Carolinas and the broader American South. The slow-smoking of whole pork shoulders is documented in Southern American barbecue culture from the nineteenth century. This oven-roasted version produces a result close to the smoked original using domestic equipment and is the standard home-cooking adaptation.

content.text: Pork shoulder (also sold as pork butt or Boston butt) is the only cut worth using here. It is heavily marbled with fat and connective tissue, which melts slowly during the long cook and produces the moist, stringy texture that distinguishes real pulled pork from a roast pork joint. A leaner cut will be dry and will not pull. Buy bone-in if you can, the bone adds flavour and the meat is easier to pull from around it.

The rub goes on the night before if possible, forming a crust that caramelises during the long roast. The pork goes into a covered casserole at 150°C for at least 6 hours, do not be tempted to raise the temperature to shorten the time. At 6 hours, check: it should feel very soft when pressed and the bone should move freely. If not, return for another hour. Rest for 30 minutes before pulling.

Mix all the dry rub ingredients together. Score the fat cap of the {{pork-shoulder}} of pork shoulder in a crosshatch pattern down to the meat. Rub the spice mixture all over the pork, working it into the scores. If time allows, cover and refrigerate overnight or for at least 2 hours.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Place the pork fat-side up in the casserole. Pour the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar and {{stock-chicken}} of stock around the base. Cover tightly and roast for 6 to 8 hours. At 6 hours, check: the pork should feel very soft when pressed through the lid and the bone should be loose. If not, return for another hour. The internal temperature should be 90°C or higher. Rest, covered, for 30 minutes before pulling.

Transfer the pork to a board. Remove and discard the bone and any large pieces of fat. Pull the meat into shreds using two forks or your hands (wearing gloves). Return the pulled pork to the casserole and mix with the cooking juices. Serve in soft rolls with coleslaw and barbecue sauce.

items.fix: Return to the oven for another hour. The change from tough to tender can seem abrupt. The meat needs to reach 90°C internal and hold there long enough for the collagen to turn into gelatin.
items.cause: Not cooked long enough; the collagen has not fully broken down
items.symptom: Pork will not pull and is still tough

items.fix: Ensure the casserole is sealed tightly; the pork should be cooking in its own steam. Add a splash more stock if the base is dry
items.cause: Too little braising liquid, or lid not sealed
items.symptom: Pork is dry rather than juicy

items.fix: Pat the pork very dry before rubbing; a wet surface prevents the spices from adhering to form a proper crust
items.cause: Surface of the pork was wet when the rub was applied
items.symptom: Rub is not forming a crust

items.fix: Be generous with the rub. It needs to season a 2 kg joint throughout; the salt in particular should feel assertive when you taste the raw spice mix
items.cause: Rub under-seasoned, or resting period too short

content.text: Pulled pork is the product of the American barbecue tradition, and the Carolinas in particular. Whole-hog and pork-shoulder barbecue has been a cultural practice there for centuries. Slow-smoking whole pigs over wood fires was recorded in the American South from colonial times. The vinegar-based sauce of the Carolinas (as opposed to the tomato-and-sugar sauce of Kansas City) reflects a food tradition rooted in specific geography. The oven version loses the smoke but keeps everything else. The result, a pile of tender, flavour-soaked pork on a soft roll with coleslaw, is one of the most satisfying things the American kitchen produces.
